request.question: Which town was the director of the "Red Curtain Trilogy" born in?

title: Red Curtain Trilogy
Baz Luhrmann
Michael O'Hare (chef)
Baby, Baby, Baby (Teresa Brewer song)
Insomnia (band)
Strictly Ballroom
Miss Utah Teen USA
Nina Ananiashvili
Kambuzia Partovi
Herons Creek, New South Wales
sentences: The Red Curtain Trilogy is the title given to the first three films directed by Baz Luhrmann:

Mark Anthony "Baz" Luhrmann ( ; born 17 September 1962) is an Australian film director, screenwriter and producer best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy", comprising his romantic comedy film "Strictly Ballroom" (1992), the romantic drama "Romeo + Juliet" (1996), and the pastiche-jukebox musical "Moulin Rouge!
His 2008 film "Australia" is an epic historical romantic drama film starring Hugh Jackman and Nicole Kidman.
His 2013 drama "The Great Gatsby", based on F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel of the same name, stars Leonardo DiCaprio (whom he also used in "Romeo + Juliet") and Tobey Maguire.
Michael O'Hare (born June 1981) is a British chef from Redcar, North Yorkshire, England.
He is chef-patron at The Man Behind The Curtain in Leeds, which was awarded a Michelin star in October 2015.
He is also Creative Director of GG Hospitality and oversees the company's restaurant The Rabbit in the Moon at the National Football Museum in Manchester.
Baby, Baby, Baby is a 1953 hit song by Teresa Brewer from the film "Those Redheads from Seattle".
The song was written in 1950 by Jerry Livingston and lyricist Mack David.

The song was sung by Brewer in the role of a singer, who appears through a red curtain line of dancing girls and commences the lyrics: "Baby, Baby, Baby love me love me do, Baby, Baby, Baby love me love true."
The song reached No.12 in the US hit parade in December 1953.

Coral released the record first in 1953 as 9-61067 with the B-side "I Guess It Was You All The Time", written by Hoagy Carmichael and Johnny Mercer, then in 1954 as an EP, EC 81086 with A2: "Jilted" Robert Colby and Dick Manning, Track B1: "Chicago Style" James Van Heusen and Johnny Burke, and B2 "My Sweetie Went Away (She Didn't Say Where, When Or Why)" written by Rey Turk, and Lou Handman.
Insomnia (Bengali: ইনসমনিয়া ) was a rock band based in Kolkata, West Bengal.
The band's repertoire included both English and Bengali songs.
The band has an album each in English ("Cry of the Spirit" - self released) and Bengali ("Proloyer Shomoye" - Asha Audio).

Insomnia were also quite adept at executing live music for theatre, being part of the act itself, in plays such as "Peacewards" (directed by Jayant Kripalani, produced by Red Curtain Productions, based on monologues written by Manjula Padmanabhan), a 3rd theatre version of "Rokto Korobi" (written by Rabindranath Tagore, conceptualised by Badal Sarkar, directed by Parnab Mukherjee, starring Sudipta Chakraborty) and numerous other plays.

"Opekkha" from their Bengali album "Proloyer Shomoye" was part of the original soundtrack of the first Bengali sex comedy movie "Aamra" (produced by Venkatesh films, directed by Mainak Bhaumik, cast - Parambrata Chatterjee, Jisshu Sengupta, Rudranil Ghosh, Nilanjana, Ananya Chatterjee, Kaushik Ghosh, Rajatava Datta, Momo).
Strictly Ballroom is a 1992 Australian romantic comedy film directed and co-written by Baz Luhrmann.
The film, Luhrmann's début, is the first in his "The Red Curtain Trilogy" of theatre-motif-related films; it was followed by "Romeo + Juliet" and "Moulin Rouge!

The Miss Utah Teen USA competition is the pageant that selects the representative for the state of Utah in the Miss Teen USA pageant.From 2006, the pageant will be produced by Red Curtain Productions, which includes a number of former Miss Utah Teen USAs and Miss Utah USAs.
Nina Ananiashvili (born: "Nino Ananiashvili", Georgian: ნინო ანანიაშვილი ; born March 28, 1963) is a Georgian ballerina and artistic director of the State Ballet of Georgia.
She has been described by the Daily Telegraph as one of the twelve greatest ballerinas of all time, and in 2002 was named Best Ballerina of the Year by the US "Dance Magazine".
Ananiashvili has been a prominent fixture of the Soviet, Russian and Georgian ballet scene for decades.

Shortly before the fall of the Iron Curtain, in 1987 the New York Magazine critic praised Nina Ananiashvili as "the best thing about the Bolshoi's Giselle whether she appeared in the title role or as the queen of the wilis."
In 2014, a film of her 1991 performance in Giselle with the Bolshoi ballet was released.
Kambuzia Partovi (also spelt Kambozia Partovi, born 11 November 1955 in Rasht) (Persian: کامبوزیا پرتوی‎ ‎ ) is an Iranian film director and scriptwriter.
He was born in Rasht, Iran on the Caspian Sea.
After studying theater arts in school he wrote mainly scripts for TV series.
In 1988 he made his feature film debut with "Golnar".
His 2007 film "Café Transit" which got the special mention at Mar del Plata film festival was selected by Iran as its candidate for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film.
He has also written screenplays for other directors, most notably Jafar Panahi's "The Circle".
Partovi trained and supported many Iranian artists and film makers, most notably Bahman Ghobadi.
In 2013 he acted in and co-directed "Closed Curtain" with Panahi.
Herons Creek is a small township on the North Coast of New South Wales, Australia co-ordinates 31°35′S 152°44′E.
The population of Herons Creek is 312 (2011).
The township is about 291 kilometres (181 miles) north of Sydney.
The North Coast railway line passes through, and a station existed at the site between 1917 and 1974.
Film director Baz Luhrmann was raised in the town.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Which town was the director of the "Red Curtain Trilogy" born in?
hop1_docs: Red Curtain Trilogy | The Red Curtain Trilogy is the title given to the first three films directed by Baz Luhrmann:

Baz Luhrmann | Mark Anthony "Baz" Luhrmann ( ; born 17 September 1962) is an Australian film director, screenwriter and producer best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy", comprising his romantic comedy film "Strictly Ballroom" (1992), the romantic drama "Romeo + Juliet" (1996), and the pastiche-jukebox musical "Moulin Rouge! " (2001).  His 2008 film "Australia" is an epic historical romantic drama film starring Hugh Jackman and Nicole Kidman.  His 2013 drama "The Great Gatsby", based on F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel of the same name, stars Leonardo DiCaprio (whom he also used in "Romeo + Juliet") and Tobey Maguire.

Strictly Ballroom | Strictly Ballroom is a 1992 Australian romantic comedy film directed and co-written by Baz Luhrmann.  The film, Luhrmann's début, is the first in his "The Red Curtain Trilogy" of theatre-motif-related films; it was followed by "Romeo + Juliet" and "Moulin Rouge! ".

The Curtain (essay) | The Curtain is a seven-part essay by Milan Kundera, along with The Art of the Novel and Testaments Betrayed composing a type of trilogy of book-length essays on the European novel.

Three Colours: Red | Three Colors: Red (French: Trois couleurs: Rouge ) is a 1994 romantic mystery film co-written, produced and directed by Polish filmmaker Krzysztof Kieślowski.  It is the final installment of "The Three Colors Trilogy", which examines the French Revolutionary ideals; it is preceded by "" and "".  Kieślowski had announced that this would be his final film, which proved true with the director's sudden death in 1996.  "Red" is about fraternity, which it examines by showing characters whose lives gradually become closely interconnected, with bonds forming between two characters who appear to have little in common.
Curtains (Red Flag song) | Curtains is a song by the American synthpop band Red Flag.  It was released as a single in 2000.  It was also included as disc V06.0 in the Megablack Box Set.

Kōhaku maku | A kōhaku maku (紅白幕 , literally red and white curtain ) is a type of decorative fabric panel used on various occasions in Japan.  The fabric takes its name from its design of vertical red and white stripes, which is known as "kōhaku".  "Kōhaku maku" panels are hung from a red and white striped rope.

- best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy" — [2] "best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy","
- Strictly Ballroom (directed by Baz Luhrmann) — [3] "directed and co-written by Baz Luhrmann."

Ambiguities:
- None identified in the passages; "Baz Luhrmann" appears to refer to a single individual with no conflicting variants.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The town (place/city) of birth for Baz Luhrmann is not given in the provided passages (place of birth / birth town missing).

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch the full biography page: "Baz Luhrmann - Wikipedia"
- Search query: "Baz Luhrmann birthplace"
- Search query: "Mark Anthony Baz Luhrmann born in" or "Baz Luhrmann early life birthplace"
- If needed, check "Red Curtain Trilogy" page for director link: "Red Curtain Trilogy - Wikipedia"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: The Great Gatsby: Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film | The Great Gatsby: Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film (also known as Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film The Great Gatsby) is the soundtrack album to the 2013 film "The Great Gatsby", an adaptation of F. Scott Fitzgerald's novel of the same name.  Interscope Records released it on May 6, 2013.  The album was produced by Baz Luhrmann and Anton Monsted, with Jay-Z serving as the album's executive producer.  The soundtrack comprises fourteen songs, including new material and cover versions performed by various artists.  It contains a mixture of genre, including hip hop, jazz, and alternative music.  Luhrmann specifically selected these styles of music to better immerse the audience into the story of "The Great Gatsby".
Moulin Rouge! Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film, Vol. 2 | Moulin Rouge!  Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film, Vol.  2

Moulin Rouge! Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film | Moulin Rouge!  Music from Baz Luhrmann's Film is a soundtrack album to Baz Luhrmann's 2001 film "Moulin Rouge! ".  It was released on May 8, 2001.  The album features most of the songs featured in the film.  However, some of the songs are alternate versions and there are two or three major songs that are left off.  The original film versions and extra songs were featured on the second soundtrack.

Strictly Ballroom (musical) | Strictly Ballroom the Musical is a musical theatre adaptation of the 1992 film "Strictly Ballroom".  It is credited as being created by Baz Luhrmann with book by Baz Luhrmann and Craig Pearce adapted by Terry Johnson.  The score consists of existing music and songs, and new musical numbers by David Foster, Sia Furler, Isaac Hasson, Bernie Herms, Baz Lurhmann, MoZella, Neff-U, Craig Pearce, Eddie Perfect, Linda Thompson and Elliot Wheeler.

Something for Everybody (Baz Luhrmann album) | Something for Everybody is an album released by Baz Luhrmann in 1998.  It contains new ambient versions (either newly recorded or remixed) of music from his films and plays, including hits from "William Shakespeare's Romeo + Juliet", "Strictly Ballroom" and "La bohème".  The most popular single from the album is the spoken word song "Everybody's Free (To Wear Sunscreen)".

Baz Luhrmann | Mark Anthony "Baz" Luhrmann ( ; born 17 September 1962) is an Australian film director, screenwriter and producer best known for "Red Curtain Trilogy", comprising his romantic comedy film "Strictly Ballroom" (1992), the romantic drama "Romeo + Juliet" (1996), and the pastiche-jukebox musical "Moulin Rouge! " (2001).  His 2008 film "Australia" is an epic historical romantic drama film starring Hugh Jackman and Nicole Kidman.  His 2013 drama "The Great Gatsby", based on F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel of the same name, stars Leonardo DiCaprio (whom he also used in "Romeo + Juliet") and Tobey Maguire.

Strictly Ballroom | Strictly Ballroom is a 1992 Australian romantic comedy film directed and co-written by Baz Luhrmann.  The film, Luhrmann's début, is the first in his "The Red Curtain Trilogy" of theatre-motif-related films; it was followed by "Romeo + Juliet" and "Moulin Rouge! ".
